Defendants Notice Of Petition And Petition To Compel Binding Arbitration, Stay State Court Proceedings, And Transfer State Court Jurisdiction To The San Diego County Court
Off Calendar Jun-22-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Stephen W. Yuen, of Heath Yuen, APC, Tel. 415.622.7064, for plaintiffs;
Shahab Fotouhi, of Fotouhi Law Group, Tel. 415.441.2470, for defendants.

Ruling: A R G U E D. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: DEFENDANT FREDRICKSON, MAZEIKA & GRANT, LLP, TIMOTHY GRANT'S Petition To Compel Binding Arbitration, Stay State Court Proceedings, And Transfer State Court Jurisdiction To The San Diego County Court is granted, the court stays this action, and the court denies the motion to transfer venue to San Diego. Defendant sufficiently establishes the existence of an arbitration agreement. See Condee v. Longwood Management Corp. (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 215, 218-219. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate unconscionability and the court severs the arbitration agreement's fee-splitting language. If defendant refuses to cover the fees, then the court will deny arbitration under Cinel v. Barna (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th 1383, 1389. In Little, 29 Cal.4th 1064, the court extended Armendariz and applied its requirements to employer-mandated arbitration of tort claims for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy (i.e., claims under Tameny v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (1980) 27 Cal.3d 167, 164). Justifying the extension, the court reasoned that Tameny claims, even though not statutory, are nevertheless "almost by definition unwaivable" (Little, at p. 1077) because they seek to enforce public policies that are carefully tethered to fundamental policies delineated in constitutional or statutory provisions. To extend the Armendariz requirements to Tameny claims was also consistent with the object of those requirements, which is "to ensure minimum standards of fairness in arbitration so that employees subject to mandatory arbitration agreements can vindicate their public rights in an arbitral forum." The same is true in this case. Plaintiff's claims include causes of action under the Labor Code to recover back wages (cause of action 13). The intentional tort (assault, battery) causes of action are subject to arbitration. The language of the parties' agreement along with the handbook is broad enough to compel arbitration of those claims and there is a public policy in favor of arbitration. Defendant correctly notes that a venue selection provision is enforceable under Battaglia v. Sup. Crt. (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 309, 317-319, where it does not conflict with the state venue statutes. Par. 7a, however, is not a venue selection clause. It states "[i]f the parties to the controversy cannot agree on three neutral arbitrators, the parties shall petition the San Diego County Superior Court for appointment of three neutral arbitrators." The agreement says nothing about where the actual arbitration shall occur. Therefore, the court grants arbitration, orders defendant to pay the extra-court fees under Armendariz, stays this case, and denies the motion to change venue. Prevailing party shall prepare form of order that complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk Felicia Green; Court Reporter Melanie Gheno, CSR No. 7489; Dept. 302. (302/EHG)
